revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Shinto_Shrine#Taisha-zukuri]]
#REDIRECT [[Shinto_shrine#Taisha-zukuri|Taisha-zukuri]]

==Structure== Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.<ref name="FK">Fujita, Koga (2008:24)</ref> In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''[[ken]]''<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|真の御柱}}).<ref name="FK"/> This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance.<ref name="jata "/> The external stairway is covered by an independent bark roof (see illustration in the gallery).
The ''honden'''s interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites.<ref name="jata "/>
Because its floor is raised, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], Shizuoka prefecture.<ref name="jata">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>

[[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|275 px|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']]{{nihongo|''Taisha-zukuri'' or ''Ōyashiro-zukuri''|大社造|}} is the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] ''honden'' style, takes its name from [[Izumo Taisha]] and, like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s, features ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> As in the case of the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.
==Structure== Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.<ref name="FK">Fujita, Koga (2008:24)</ref> In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''[[ken]]''<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|真の御柱}}).<ref name="FK"/> This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance.<ref name="jata "/> The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery).
The ''honden'''s interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites.<ref name="jata "/>
Because its floor is raised, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], Shizuoka prefecture.<ref name="jata">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>

Named after [[Izumo Taisha]], like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> As in the case of the ''shinmei-zukuri'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.
==Structure== Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.<ref name="FK">Fujita, Koga (2008:24)</ref> In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''[[ken]]''<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}).<ref name="FK"/> This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance.<ref name="jata "/> The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery).
The ''honden'''s interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites.<ref name="jata "/>
Because its floor is raised, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], Shizuoka prefecture.<ref name="jata">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>

[[ja:大社造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|275 px|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']]{{nihongo|'''Taisha-zukuri''' or '''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''|大社造|}} is the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] style. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> Like the ''shinmei-zukuri'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.
==Structure== [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' during the [[Kamakura period]]]]Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.<ref name="FK">Fujita, Koga (2008:24)</ref> In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''[[ken]]''<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}).<ref name="FK"/> This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance.<ref name="jata "/> The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery).
The ''honden'''s interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites.<ref name="jata "/>
Because its floor is raised, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].<ref name="jata">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.

In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}).<ref name="FK"/> This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance.<ref name="jata "/> The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery).
The ''honden'''s interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites.<ref name="jata "/>
Because its floor is raised, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].<ref name="jata">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>

Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> Like the ''shinmei-zukuri'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.
==Structure== [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' during the [[Kamakura period]]]]Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.<ref name="FK">Fujita, Koga (2008:24)</ref> In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''[[ken]]''<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}).<ref name="FK"/> This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance.<ref name="jata "/> The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery).
[[File:Kamosu_jinja.jpg|thumb|Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'']]The ''honden'''s interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites.<ref name="jata"/>
Because its floor is raised, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].<ref name="jata">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref name="FK"/> Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction.<ref name="FK"/>

In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}).<ref name="FK"/> This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance.<ref name="jata "/> The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery).

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.
==Structure== [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' during the [[Kamakura period]]]]Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.<ref name="FK">Fujita, Koga (2008:24)</ref> In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''[[ken]]''<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}).<ref name="FK"/> This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance.<ref name="jata "/> The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery).
[[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']]The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites.<ref name="jata"/>
Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].<ref name="jata">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref name="FK"/> Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction.<ref name="FK"/>

[[File:Kamosu_jinja.jpg|thumb|220 px|Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'']]{{nihongo|'''Taisha-zukuri''' or '''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''|大社造|}} is the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] style. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> The floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''shinmei-zukuri'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.
==Structure== [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' during the [[Kamakura period]]]]Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.<ref name="FK">Fujita, Koga (2008:24)</ref> In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''[[ken]]''<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites.<ref name="jata"/>
Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].<ref name="jata">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>

==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=144377281X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0804838380 }} {{Shinto shrine}} [[ja:大社造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|thumb|220 px|Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'']]{{nihongo|'''Taisha-zukuri''' or '''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''|大社造|}} is the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] style. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]].
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.
==Structure== [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' during the [[Kamakura period]]]]Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.<ref name="FK">Fujita, Koga (2008:24)</ref> In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''[[ken]]''<ref>A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m.</ref> in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''tsumairi-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}).<ref name="FK"/> This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance.<ref name="jata "/> The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery).
[[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']]The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites.<ref name="jata"/>
Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].<ref name="jata">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref name="FK"/> Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction.<ref name="FK"/>

{{nihongo|'''Taisha-zukuri''' or '''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''|大社造|}} is the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] style. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]]. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' during the [[Kamakura period]]]]Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''[[ken]]''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']]The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=144377281X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0804838380 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[ja:大社造]]

Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[fr:Taisha-zukuri]] [[ja:大社造]]

revision.text:
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a dwelling at Toro
{{nihongo|'''Taisha-zukuri''' or '''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''|大社造|}} is the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] style. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]]. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' during the [[Kamakura period]]]]Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']]The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[fr:Taisha-zukuri]] [[ja:大社造]]

revision.comment: Corr. after article [[Izumo-taisha]]. But better sources would be good.
revision.text:
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a dwelling at Toro
{{nihongo|'''Taisha-zukuri''' or '''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''|大社造|}} is the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] style. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]]. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]][[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]]Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 mm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']]The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[fr:Taisha-zukuri]] [[ja:大社造]]

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

{{nihongo|'''Taisha-zukuri''' or '''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''|大社造|}} is the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]]. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]]

{{italic title}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival in Japan of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]]. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''chigi'' (vertical) and ''katsuogi'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by 15 [[tatami]] (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]]

It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]]

{{italic title}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=Japanese}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=1-4437-7281-X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0-8048-3838-0 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]]
