Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri|*]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Taisha-zukuri]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taisha-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Taisha-zukuri]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taisha-zukuri]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Taisha-zukuri]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] {{Izumo-taishakyo}}

{{Short description|Type of Shinto shrine}} {{italic title}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri|*]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Taisha-zukuri]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taisha-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Taisha-zukuri]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taisha-zukuri]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Taisha-zukuri]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] {{Izumo-taishakyo}}

{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David {{Short description|Type of Shinto shrine}} {{italic title}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David {{Short description|Type of Shinto shrine}} {{italic title}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David {{Short description|Type of Shinto shrine}} {{italic title}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 {{Short description|Type of Shinto shrine}} {{italic title}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 {{Short description|Type of Shinto shrine}} {{italic title}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]] {{Izumo-taishakyo}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri|*]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Taisha-zukuri]] [[en:Taisha-zukuri]] [[ja:Taisha-zukuri]]

{{Short description|Type of Shinto shrine}} {{italic title}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kamosu jinja.jpg|alt=Kamosu Jinja's ''honden''|x220px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Toro1.jpg|alt=A reconstructed dwelling at Toro|x220px]] |}
Kamosu Jinja's ''honden'' and a granary at Toro
{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]] and the oldest [[Shinto shrine]] [[Shinto architecture|architectural style]]. Named after [[Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] in Japan. ==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the [[Kamakura period]]]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''[[tsumairi]]-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''[[Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in [[Toro (archaeological site)|Toro]], [[Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue, Shimane prefecture]], built in 1582 and now declared a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]] {{Izumo-taishakyo}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri|*]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Taisha-zukuri]] [[en:Taisha-zukuri]] [[ja:Taisha-zukuri]]
