{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style}} and the oldest {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style}}. Named after [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi}}'' and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi}}'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism}} in Japan. ==History== Ancient {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines}} were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi}}-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro}}, [[:en:Shizuoka prefecture|Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue, Shimane prefecture}}, built in 1582 and now declared a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Izumo-taishakyo}} [[en:Taisha-zukuri]] [[fr:Taisha-zukuri]] [[ja:大社造]] [[ru:Тайся-дзукури]] [[zh:大社造]] {{wikidata link|Q387874}} [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri|*]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

{{nihongo|'''''Taisha-zukuri''''' or '''''Ōyashiro-zukuri'''''|大社造|}} is an ancient Japanese {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style|1=Shinto architecture}} and the oldest {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style|1=Shinto architecture}}. Named after [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]]'s ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary), like [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' style it features a bark roof decorated with poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}'' and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}'', plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar (''shin no mihashira'').Encyclopedia of Shinto The ''honden's'' floor is raised above the ground through the use of stilts (see photo). Like the ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, it predates the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}} in Japan. ==History== Ancient {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])Young & Young (2007:50)Kishida (2008:33) or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724) The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[W:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark. Such early shrines did not include a space for worship. Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''taisha-zukuri'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''.Kishida (2008:34) They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]Kishida (2008:35) respectively and date to before 552.Kishida (2008:126) According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day. ==Structure== [[Image:Izumo-taisha scale model 121281969 6127ff6b17 o.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Reconstruction model of the ancient Izumo-taisha honden, based on remains of old pillars found on the site.]] [[File:Izumooyashiro11.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Reconstruction of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' somewhat later, during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}]] Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' over time has gone through profound changes that have greatly decreased its size and changed its structure.Fujita, Koga (2008:24) In its present form, it is a gabled building 2x2 ''ken''A ''ken'' is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building. In this case, a ''ken'' is 6.32 m, well above its standard value of just below 2 m. in size, with an entrance on the gabled end (a characteristic called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|WD=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi|1=Tsumairi}}-zukuri''|妻入造}}. Like Ise Grand Shrine's, it has purely ornamental poles called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal) on a cypress bark-covered roof, plus archaic features like gable-end pillars and a single central pillar ({{nihongo|''shin no mihashira''|心の御柱}}). This pillar has a diameter of 10.9 cm, has no obvious structural role and is believed to have had a purely religious significance. The external stairway is covered by an independent bark-covered roof (see illustration in the gallery). [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|Izumo Taisha's ''honden'']] The ''honden's'' interior is a square divided into four identical sections, each covered by fifteen ''[[tatami]]'' (straw mats). The floor plan has therefore the shape of the Chinese character for {{nihongo|rice field|田}}, an element which suggests a possible connection with harvest propitiation rites. Because its floor is raised above the ground, the ''honden'' is believed to have its origin in raised-floor granaries like those found in {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro|1=Toro (archaeological site)}}, [[:en:Shizuoka prefecture|Shizuoka prefecture]].JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/taishazukuri.htm Taisha-zukuri], accessed on December 1, 2009 The oldest extant example of ''taisha-zukuri'' is the ''honden'' at Kamosu Shrine in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue, Shimane prefecture|1=Matsue}}, built in 1582 and now declared a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. Smaller than Izumo Taisha's, it nonetheless has thick supporting pillars. It is deeper, has a higher floor, and differs significantly from Izumo Taisha's. It probably represents an older style of construction. {{clear}} ==Gallery== Image:Izumo Shrine Honden.jpg|Front and side view of Izumo Taisha's ''honden'' Image:Izumo-shrine Haiden Honden.jpg|The ''honden'' is visible in the background File:Izumo-taisha 121538651 f69f257ed0 o.jpg|Roofs at Izumo Taisha ==Notes== ==References== *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book|title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi|editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku|publisher=Shōwa-dō|date=April 10, 1990|edition=September 30, 2008|isbn=4-8122-9805-9|language=ja}} *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Izumo-taishakyo}} [[en:Taisha-zukuri]] [[fr:Taisha-zukuri]] [[ja:大社造]] [[ru:Тайся-дзукури]] [[zh:大社造]] {{wikidata link|Q387874}} [[Category:Izumo Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri|*]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
